What Are Claude Code Hooks? Auto-Run Lint, Block rm -rf, Inject Context on SessionStart — A Complete Guide
Claude Code Hooks are "automatic trigger points" in Claude Code's lifecycle — they fire your Shell command at the moment you specify. The common ones are
(every time you send a prompt), and
(when a session opens). You just write the config into the
After using Claude Code for a while, you've probably hit a situation like this: every time it edits a file, you have to remind it to run Prettier and ESLint again (sure, you could just run
yourself, but humans are lazy). Or you wanted to go fast so you flipped on
, but now you're nervous it might fire off something like
. Or your Context got Compacted and Claude completely forgot some important project conventions you'd already explained, so you have to explain them again — ugh.

So this post is about a really important Claude Code technique: Hooks. They let you "hook into" Claude Code's lifecycle and trigger automated actions, making sure it does the right thing at the right moment.

which covers the layering concept of
. This post is a follow-up that pulls the hooks feature buried in settings out into its own deep dive.
What's the difference between Hooks, Skills, CLAUDE.md, and Rules?
Before getting into how to set up hooks, let's quickly compare it against the other "teach Claude what to do" mechanisms, so it's clearer what each one is actually for.
Loaded on startup OR when relevant paths are touched
Modular rules that can fire by path
Claude decides on its own when to use
Bundling an entire workflow into a reusable capability
If the table didn't quite click, think of it this way:
Skill is a "capability" — these three all rely on Claude reading them and choosing to follow
Hook is different — hooks are run by Claude Code itself,
, the moment the trigger fires the command will run

Here's a concrete example. Say in CLAUDE.md you write "run Prettier after every file edit". Most of the time Claude listens, but occasionally it forgets (Context might've blown up?!). If you use a PostToolUse hook to set "run Prettier automatically after every
", then regardless of whether Claude feels like it, your Shell command is guaranteed to run.
So hooks fit the scenario of...
Things I absolutely cannot tolerate Claude missing
Like lint, format, security guardrails. The fuzzier stuff that needs Claude's judgment is still better off in CLAUDE.md.

Where does the config file go?
Like other Claude Code settings, hooks also have layers:
Won't end up in the project repo
In short: hooks the whole team should have (like lint) go in
, personal preferences (like desktop notifications) go in
, and sensitive-path local rules go in
Now we get to the main event of this post: "what events can we actually hook into?"
Honestly, Claude Code offers more than 20 events. Listing them all would scare people off, so I'll group them by "how often I actually use them in real work".
Here's a diagram of a typical Claude Code session lifecycle, with the events that fire on each arrow — you can match it up with where each hook fits:
Diagram showing when Claude Code Hooks events fire
When you press Enter to send a prompt
Inject extra context, filter sensitive info
When a session opens or resumes
Load project context, output git status
When Claude finishes responding and is about to stop
Verify the task was really completed
These six are the most frequently used — most real-world examples revolve around them.
Other ones that are rarer but worth knowing
When a permission dialog is about to appear
When auto mode blocks a tool call
When a subagent finishes a task
Back up the conversation, reinject rules after compaction
Clean up temp files, write logs
When a settings file is modified during the session
When the working directory or a watched file changes

At this point, you should see that hooks pretty much cover every node Claude Code passes through. Almost anything you can imagine in the shape of "I want X to run automatically when Y happens" is doable — the concept is the same as the "lifecycle events" you'd find in a framework.
How do you write a matcher?
Time to talk about how to actually write the hook condition.
You saw the hook example earlier looks like this:
decides "under what conditions this hook group activates", and what it compares against depends on the event. For the commonly used
, it compares against the "tool name", so the most intuitive way to write it is to just drop the tool name into the
Here's a concrete example — this hook is "before Claude runs Bash, show a reminder in the terminal":
Screenshot showing the 'Bash is about to run' reminder
, meaning "this hook only fires when Claude uses the Bash tool".
? Because Claude Code talks to hooks in "JSON format". If you just write
, what Claude Code receives is unstructured plain text — it doesn't know what you want it to do, so it just treats it as an execution log entry and Claude never sees it. But
jq -n '{ systemMessage: "Bash is about to run" }'

produces a JSON output, which is basically telling Claude Code "this field is called systemMessage, please show its contents to Claude". When Claude Code sees that recognized format, it actually feeds the message in.
If you want to watch multiple tools at once, separate them with

Finally, if you want to "trigger no matter what tool runs", just leave
This hook appends the tool name to a log file before any tool is used by Claude — basically logging every tool call across the whole session.

Advanced: use regex to match MCP tools
The remaining 5% is when you want to match tools provided by an MCP server. MCP tools are named like

If you want to watch every tool from a given MCP server at once, use the regex
, followed by anything". Claude Code's rule for when to use regex is simple: if the matcher is made of anything other than letters, digits,
, it runs as a JavaScript regex; otherwise it's compared as a literal string. So earlier writings like
won't accidentally be treated as regex.
Not all events match on tool name
Everything above was tool-related events, but some events don't match on tool name — they match on some other "category of the event itself".

"echo 'Reminder: use pnpm for package management, not npm'"

The difference between the two filter fields:
is the first-level coarse filter: "only look at the Bash tool".
is the second-level fine filter — it also matches the actual command Bash is about to run.
is the "anything after this" wildcard, using the same syntax as the permissions settings.
The biggest upside is "the hook subprocess only spins up when the condition is actually matched". Compared to firing the hook every time and then using
to parse arguments to decide what to do, the performance is way better.
field was added later, so older versions of Claude Code might not recognize it and just ignore it. If you wrote
but the hook behaves as if it isn't there, run
first to check your version and update.
How does a hook talk to Claude Code?
Now that matcher makes sense, let's look at how a hook actually "exchanges messages" with Claude Code. Here's the smallest possible example:
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

#!/bin/bash

# 1. Capture the JSON Claude Code sent into the INPUT variable
INPUT=$(cat)

# 2. Output a message back to Claude Code — this doesn't show up in your terminal,
#    Claude Code picks it up as the hook's response
echo "Hello Claude"

# 3. Exit the script. Returning 0 means "all good, continue".
#    If you write `exit 2`, that means "something's wrong, block this action".
exit 0
# 1. Capture the JSON Claude Code sent into the INPUT variable
# 2. Output a message back to Claude Code — this doesn't show up in your terminal,
#    Claude Code picks it up as the hook's response
# 3. Exit the script. Returning 0 means "all good, continue".
#    If you write `exit 2`, that means "something's wrong, block this action".
example: when Claude wants to run
, your hook receives a JSON payload like this on stdin:
A lot of people writing their first hook trip up here: they assume
— any other non-zero exit code doesn't.
Using JSON output for finer control
your only options? Not quite — you can do more granular things (like auto-approving, or directly rewriting the command Claude is about to run). The trick is
plus outputting a JSON payload to stdout:
: skip the permission prompt and allow directly
: block the action and feed the reason back to Claude
: fall back to the normal permission flow and prompt you
: the hook has no opinion, go back to the normal permission flow (same effect as not writing this field)
Don't mix exit 2 and JSON output. When Claude Code sees exit 2, it reads stderr as the reason and ignores any JSON on stdout. Pick one approach: either exit 2 with stderr, or exit 0 with JSON.
All the examples so far used
, which is the most common form. But Claude Code has four others:
POST event data to a URL
Hooking into a remote service for shared auditing or org-wide validation
Call a tool on a connected MCP server
Reusing existing MCP tools as hook logic
Hand data to a Claude model to decide
Situations needing semantic judgment instead of hard-coded rules
Dispatch a subagent with tools to verify
Situations where you need to actually read files or run commands to decide

hook example. Say you want another model to check whether Claude really finished the requested task after it stops talking:
"Check whether Claude actually completed every requested task. If not, reply {

When this hook fires, Claude Code automatically calls a model to do a single-turn yes/no decision. If it replies that the task wasn't finished, the main Claude keeps going — basically a free quality-control checkpoint.

This hook is tied to the project's lint/format rules, so put it in

It's easier to maintain hooks as standalone Shell scripts. Start by creating

#!/bin/bash
INPUT=$(cat)
COMMAND=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.tool_input.command')

# Only block actual rm -rf / or rm -r /. Other paths (like /tmp/foo) are allowed through
if echo "$COMMAND" | grep -qE 'rm[[:space:]]+-rf?[[:space:]]+/([[:space:]]|$)'; then
  echo "Blocked: rm -rf from root is not allowed" >&2
  exit 2
fi

exit 0
# Only block actual rm -rf / or rm -r /. Other paths (like /tmp/foo) are allowed through
"Blocked: rm -rf from root is not allowed"
I'm using the POSIX character class
is a GNU grep extension — macOS's default BSD grep doesn't necessarily support it, which would silently break the match.
is the POSIX standard syntax and works in every grep on Mac and Linux.
to be followed by whitespace or end-of-line, so:
This pattern is intentionally conservative — it only blocks literal
. If you want to go harder (like blocking
and other variants), follow the same model and chain more
checks, each one with its own
Don't forget to make it executable:
is a built-in Claude Code environment variable — it expands to the project root, safer than hardcoding an absolute path.
runs before the permission mode check, so hooks are one of the few mechanisms that can actually block actions in "bypass permissions" mode.

If you want to inject dynamic content (like the current git branch or uncommitted files), swap the

#!/bin/bash
BRANCH=$(git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD 2>/dev/null)
DIRTY=$(git status --porcelain | wc -l)
echo "Current branch: $BRANCH, uncommitted files: $DIRTY"
This hook is best placed in

, because "use pnpm" and "run pnpm test before commit" are internal project conventions — committing it means everyone who clones the repo picks it up automatically, no one has to set it up themselves.
Get a notification when Claude is waiting for your permission or your next prompt, so you can confidently go do other things:
On Mac the first run might not show anything, because

sends notifications through the built-in Script Editor app. You need to go to System Settings → Notifications, find Script Editor, and enable its notification permission. If Script Editor isn't even showing up in the list, run
once in Terminal first — that will make it appear in the notification settings list.
Desktop notifications are purely personal preference — put this in
Send every Claude action to Discord
If you don't want to babysit the terminal, or you want to share what Claude is up to with the team via a Discord channel, you can use a PreToolUse hook to call a Discord webhook.
First, generate a webhook URL in your Discord channel settings, then export it in your shell profile (don't hardcode it in the script — committing it to a repo basically leaks it):
language-javascript shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
// Read the JSON Claude Code sent in via stdin
// Build a Discord embed so the message looks nicer
(using personal settings so it works across all projects):
means "notify on any tool", so no matter what Claude is about to do —
— you'll get a Discord message. If you want different emoji, colors, or fields per tool type, extend along these lines.
This whole hook is for personal notifications and the webhook URL is yours alone — put it in
at the personal layer, no need to commit.
The webhook URL should always be read from an environment variable, never hardcoded in the script. If you really want to share one webhook with the team, the hook itself can live in
, but the webhook URL should still only live in each person's shell profile or

When you set multiple hooks on the same event, they all
— they don't block each other, and Claude Code combines all the results at the end. For
, the permission decision follows the "strictest wins" rule: deny beats ask, ask beats allow.
A concrete example — you can set both "log every Bash command" and "block rm -rf" at the same time:
, both hooks fire at the same time — the log gets written, the rm gets blocked. The log doesn't miss the entry just because rm was blocked, because the two hooks run independently.
How do you confirm a hook actually ran?
You've set up a hook but aren't sure if it's even firing? Claude Code gives you two ways to check.
inside Claude Code to bring up a list of currently configured hooks, grouped by event, each one drillable to see its matcher and command.
is read-only — to actually edit a hook, you still need to edit
The second is the debug log. When you launch, add
Then open another window and run
— every hook trigger will show its full stdout, stderr, and exit code in the log. If you only want hook-related output without other debug noise drowning it out, use
: once you've nailed down the layering of

Advanced: use if to filter on tool arguments too

When you're developing with Claude Code, do you find yourself constantly telling it to run Prettier again? Or worrying that one day it'll accidentally fire off rm -rf? This post walks through how Claude Code Hooks work — when events like PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, SessionStart, Notification, and Stop fire, how to set up matcher and command in settings.json, how to block actions with exit code 2 — and runs through practical hooks: auto-format, blocking rm -rf, reinjecting project context after Compact, Mac desktop notifications, plus prompt / agent hook types and /hooks debugging.
